steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Left of blank: independent clause — "Lucia Macchia...studies socioeconomic [inequality]." Right of blank: independent clause — "other researchers investigate areas such as organizational behavior and personal finance." The connector "for instance" is a conjunctive adverb. Rule: a conjunctive adverb between two independent clauses takes a comma before it and a semicolon after it (or a semicolon before and comma after).

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — inequality, for instance;: "...studies socioeconomic inequality, for instance; other researchers investigate..." Comma before "for instance" sets it off as a parenthetical; semicolon after separates the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice B — inequality; for instance,: “...studies socioeconomic inequality; for instance, other researchers investigate organizational behavior and personal finance.” Here the semicolon falls before “for instance,” making the entire second clause (“other researchers investigate organizational behavior and personal finance”) an instance of Macchia studying inequality — which is illogical; other researchers’ different fields cannot be examples of Macchia’s own work. Choice A places the semicolon after “for instance,” so “for instance” is a parenthetical marking Macchia as one example of a researcher, and the semicolon then introduces a parallel statement about other researchers in different fields — logically and grammatically sound. ✗
  • Choice C — inequality, for instance,: "...studies socioeconomic inequality, for instance, other researchers investigate..." Two commas create a comma splice — the two independent clauses are joined only by commas without a semicolon. ✗
  • Choice D — inequality for instance;: "...studies socioeconomic inequality for instance; other researchers..." No comma before "for instance" leaves it unset as a parenthetical, creating a run-on up to the semicolon. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
